body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

`full-lifecycle-toolcall` maps to coverage_id `auto-executed-tool-call` (scenario-meanings.md): the agent invokes one or more tools mid-turn, every call auto-resolves with no user approval, and the turn completes — the user-observable arc is `ready → working → ready` and state NEVER enters `waiting`. Its primitive is `tool_calls`; it requires `headless_mode` and `tool_calls`.

**agent_supports: yes.** Pi invokes its bash/shell tool autonomously and runs it without an approval gate. A headless `pi --print -p <prompt>` invocation that asks pi to run a shell command produces a real tool round-trip that auto-resolves without ever blocking on the user, then completes the turn and exits. The coverage matrix records pi/auto-executed-tool-call as yes/yes.

**daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready.** The pi parser translates `toolCall` / `toolResult` message events into the tailer's tool-use / tool-result records and keeps the session `working` across them; the final assistant message's `stopReason=stop` maps to `turn_done`, settling `working → ready` via the primary turn-end path. Pi has no permission hooks, so no `waiting` transition is emitted — matching the scenario's negative invariant. Confirmed against the committed pi regression fixture for this cell: the transcript carries one `toolCall` + two `toolResult` events and the events arc is `ready → working → ready` with zero `waiting` transitions. The sibling pi/basic-turn cell (committed this session) exercises the identical headless launch + working→ready arc and passes; full-lifecycle-toolcall adds the tool round-trip inside the working span.

## Recipe-shape note (headless)

Recorded with the HEADLESS pi driver (`pi --print -p <prompt>`), same shape as basic-turn. Pi writes one JSONL session file per invocation on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy), so the daemon's first scanner tick keys the real UUID transcript directly — the curated fixture is a SINGLE rollout session with no separately-matchable proc-<PID> pre-session phase. The prompt must actually elicit a tool call (it asks pi to run `echo hello` via its bash tool, then reply `done`), so the working span sustains a real tool round-trip. The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the tool call is asserted via the working span it sustains plus the transcript's toolCall/toolResult events, not a dedicated phase.
caveats: claudecode's variant of this scenario ends in waiting via a PostToolUse permission hook (the top-level scenarios.json verify.has_waiting_state is claudecode-shaped); pi has no such hook, so the pi arc is ready→working→ready and the spec asserts a no-waiting invariant rather than has_waiting_state.
Pi cannot accept a pre-assigned UUID — it generates one itself. The headless driver discovers the UUID from the session header on line 1 of the freshly-created transcript.

`pi --print` exits after the single turn, so the rollout session's terminal working→ready settle and process_exited can land close together; the spec anchors turn_end to turn_start (not session_birth) so the matcher picks the post-turn ready, not the birth ready.

sources.note: Identity.Name=pi, Source variant FilesUnderRoot under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/, ExactName{Name: "pi"} process matcher.
toolCall/toolResult → tool-use/tool-result; stopReason=stop on the final assistant event maps to turn_done (working→ready). No permission gate → no waiting.
Carries 1 toolCall + 2 toolResult events; events arc is ready→working→ready with no waiting transition.
auto-executed-tool-call canonical flow: ready→working→ready, never enters waiting.
Headless `pi --print -p <prompt>` single-shot driver; resolves the freshly-created transcript and extracts the UUID from line 1.
